texts.label: text
texts.orig: Reshma lives in a coastal town of Kerala and is very fond of writing short stories. Her grandmother loves listening to her stories, so Reshma was writing a new story to share with her grandmother on her 60th birthday.
texts.text: Reshma lives in a coastal town of Kerala and is very fond of writing short stories. Her grandmother loves listening to her stories, so Reshma was writing a new story to share with her grandmother on her 60th birthday.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The story was based ona ship carrying spices from Kerala for trade in the olden days. Reshma was aware that in those days, the sailors used stars to find directions at night. But in her story, a situation arose wherein the sailors got caught in a storm with an overcast sky and stars
texts.text: The story was based ona ship carrying spices from Kerala for trade in the olden days. Reshma was aware that in those days, the sailors used stars to find directions at night. But in her story, a situation arose wherein the sailors got caught in a storm with an overcast sky and stars were not visible. Reshma could not take her story forward as she could not think of a way for sailors to find directions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: were not visible. Reshma could not take her story forward as she could not think of a way for sailors to find directions.
texts.text: were not visible. Reshma could not take her story forward as she could not think of a way for sailors to find directions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: She searched for information on the internet and her school library. She learnt that the travellers used a device, known as a magnetic compass, for finding directions.
texts.text: She searched for information on the internet and her school library. She learnt that the travellers used a device, known as a magnetic compass, for finding directions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Reshma had seen pencil boxes and purses which had magnets to keep them closed. A writing board in her school also had a duster with a magnet. But she had never looked at those carefully. She now became curious to learn more about magnets and magnetic compasses.
texts.text: Reshma had seen pencil boxes and purses which had magnets to keep them closed. A writing board in her school also had a duster with a magnet. But she had never looked at those carefully. She now became curious to learn more about magnets and magnetic compasses.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4.1: Some common items that have magnets attached to them
texts.text: Fig. 4.1: Some common items that have magnets attached to them

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The magnets used by sailors in the olden days were based on naturally occurring magnets, known as lodestones which were discovered in ancient times. Later on, people found out that magnets could also be made from pieces of iron. Nowadays, we have magnets made of different materials. The magnets that you find in your school laboratory and those used in pencil boxes, stickers, toys are all artificial magnets (Fig. 4.1). The magnets can be of various shapes, some of which are shown in Fig. 4.2.
texts.text: The magnets used by sailors in the olden days were based on naturally occurring magnets, known as lodestones which were discovered in ancient times. Later on, people found out that magnets could also be made from pieces of iron. Nowadays, we have magnets made of different materials. The magnets that you find in your school laboratory and those used in pencil boxes, stickers, toys are all artificial magnets (Fig. 4.1). The magnets can be of various shapes, some of which are shown in Fig. 4.2.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4.2: Magnets of different shapes
texts.text: Fig. 4.2: Magnets of different shapes

texts.text: Was your prediction correct for all objects? Which materials stuck to the magnet? What conclusion can you draw?

texts.orig: Through this activity, we found out that some of the objects were attracted to the magnet and stuck to it, while others were not. The materials which are attracted towards
texts.text: Through this activity, we found out that some of the objects were attracted to the magnet and stuck to it, while others were not. The materials which are attracted towards

texts.text: Do all parts of a magnet attract magnetic materials equally?

texts.text: Which materials listed in Table 4.1 were found to be non-magnetic?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4 2 Poles of Magnet
texts.text: 4 2 Poles of Magnet

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 4.2: Let us investigate
texts.text: Activity 4.2: Let us investigate

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Spread some iron filings (very small pieces of iron) ona sheet of paper.
texts.text: ¢ Spread some iron filings (very small pieces of iron) ona sheet of paper.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Place a bar magnet over them. Tap the paper and observe carefully what happens to the iron filings.
texts.text: Place a bar magnet over them. Tap the paper and observe carefully what happens to the iron filings.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Do you observe anything special about the way they stick to the magnet? Do the iron filings stick all over the magnet uniformly? Or do the iron filings stick more at some places?
texts.text: Do you observe anything special about the way they stick to the magnet? Do the iron filings stick all over the magnet uniformly? Or do the iron filings stick more at some places?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We find that maximum iron filings stick near the ends of the bar magnet, as shown in Fig. 4.4, while a very few iron filings stick at the remaining part of the magnet.
texts.text: We find that maximum iron filings stick near the ends of the bar magnet, as shown in Fig. 4.4, while a very few iron filings stick at the remaining part of the magnet.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4.4: Iron filings sticking to a bar magnet
texts.text: Fig. 4.4: Iron filings sticking to a bar magnet

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If we repeat this activity with magnets of other shapes, do we get the same result?
texts.text: If we repeat this activity with magnets of other shapes, do we get the same result?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These ends of the magnet are called the two poles of the magnet—the North pole and the South pole. Most of the iron filings stick to the poles of a magnet of any shape.
texts.text: These ends of the magnet are called the two poles of the magnet—the North pole and the South pole. Most of the iron filings stick to the poles of a magnet of any shape.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is not possible to obtain a magnet with a single pole. If a magnet is broken into smaller pieces, North and South poles always
texts.text: It is not possible to obtain a magnet with a single pole. If a magnet is broken into smaller pieces, North and South poles always

texts.label: text
texts.orig: exist in pairs even in the smallest piece of the magnet. A single North pole or a South pole cannot exist.
texts.text: exist in pairs even in the smallest piece of the magnet. A single North pole or a South pole cannot exist.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3 Finding Directions
texts.text: 4.3 Finding Directions

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 4.3: Let us experiment
texts.text: Activity 4.3: Let us experiment

texts.label: text
texts.orig: —_&z<CCii~ESs~_____o
texts.text: —_&z<CCii~ESs~_____o

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Suspend a bar magnet with a thread tied to the middle of the magnet as shown in Fig. 4.5. You may need to adjust the position of the string till the magnet is balanced horizontally.
texts.text: ¢ Suspend a bar magnet with a thread tied to the middle of the magnet as shown in Fig. 4.5. You may need to adjust the position of the string till the magnet is balanced horizontally.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Now rotate the magnet gently in the horizontal direction and let it come to rest.
texts.text: @ Now rotate the magnet gently in the horizontal direction and let it come to rest.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Mark the position corresponding to the ends of the magnet on the ground (or on a piece of paper stuck to the ground). Join these two points on the ground with a line. This line indicates the direction along which the magnet comes to rest.
texts.text: ¢ Mark the position corresponding to the ends of the magnet on the ground (or on a piece of paper stuck to the ground). Join these two points on the ground with a line. This line indicates the direction along which the magnet comes to rest.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Now again rotate the magnet by giving a gentle push at its one end and wait till it comes to rest. Does the magnet rest along the same line?
texts.text: ¢ Now again rotate the magnet by giving a gentle push at its one end and wait till it comes to rest. Does the magnet rest along the same line?

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4.5: A freely suspended bar magnet
texts.text: Fig. 4.5: A freely suspended bar magnet

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If we have noticed the direction where the Sun rises or sets, we have an approximate idea of where East or West is. Hence, we can locate the direction along which the magnet rests.
texts.text: If we have noticed the direction where the Sun rises or sets, we have an approximate idea of where East or West is. Hence, we can locate the direction along which the magnet rests.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A freely suspended magnet comes to rest along the north-south direction. The end of the magnet that points towards north direction is called the North-seeking pole or the North pole of the magnet. The other end that points towards the South direction is called the South-seeking pole or the South pole of the magnet. A freely suspended magnet rests along the north-south direction because our Earth itself behaves like a giant magnet.
texts.text: A freely suspended magnet comes to rest along the north-south direction. The end of the magnet that points towards north direction is called the North-seeking pole or the North pole of the magnet. The other end that points towards the South direction is called the South-seeking pole or the South pole of the magnet. A freely suspended magnet rests along the north-south direction because our Earth itself behaves like a giant magnet.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Repeat this activity with a small iron bar in place of the bar magnet. What do you observe? Does it always rest along north-south direction? It does not. It can rest along any direction. This implies that only magnets rest along north-south direction. This activity provides us with a way to test whether a piece of metal is a magnet or not.
texts.text: Repeat this activity with a small iron bar in place of the bar magnet. What do you observe? Does it always rest along north-south direction? It does not. It can rest along any direction. This implies that only magnets rest along north-south direction. This activity provides us with a way to test whether a piece of metal is a magnet or not.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The property of a freely suspended magnet to always rest along the north-south direction is used to find directions. Based on this, a small device called a magnetic compass was
texts.text: The property of a freely suspended magnet to always rest along the north-south direction is used to find directions. Based on this, a small device called a magnetic compass was developed in olden days for finding directions. It has a magnet in the shape of a needle which can rotate freely (Fig. 4.6). The needle of a magnetic compass indicates the north-south direction.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: developed in olden days for finding directions. It has a magnet in the shape of a needle which can rotate freely (Fig. 4.6). The needle of a magnetic compass indicates the north-south direction.
texts.text: developed in olden days for finding directions. It has a magnet in the shape of a needle which can rotate freely (Fig. 4.6). The needle of a magnetic compass indicates the north-south direction.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4.6: A magnetic compass
texts.text: Fig. 4.6: A magnetic compass

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The compass is kept at the place where we wish to know the directions. After some time, the needle comes to rest in the north-south direction. The compass box is then gently rotated until the north and south marked on the dial are aligned with the needle. Now all directions at that place are as indicated on the dial.
texts.text: The compass is kept at the place where we wish to know the directions. After some time, the needle comes to rest in the north-south direction. The compass box is then gently rotated until the north and south marked on the dial are aligned with the needle. Now all directions at that place are as indicated on the dial.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: How can we make our own magnetic compass?
texts.text: How can we make our own magnetic compass?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 4.4: Let us construct
texts.text: Activity 4.4: Let us construct

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Collect a few materials like a cork piece, iron sewing needle, a permanent bar magnet, a glass bowl, and water.
texts.text: @ Collect a few materials like a cork piece, iron sewing needle, a permanent bar magnet, a glass bowl, and water.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Place the iron sewing needle on a wooden table. Then keep any one pole of the magnet at one end of the needle. Move the magnet over the needle ae along its length as shown in Fig. 4.7a. When it reaches the other end of the needle, lift it up.
texts.text: @ Place the iron sewing needle on a wooden table. Then keep any one pole of the magnet at one end of the needle. Move the magnet over the needle ae along its length as shown in Fig. 4.7a. When it reaches the other end of the needle, lift it up.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Bring the same pole of the magnet you started with to the same end of the sewing needle from which you began, and repeat the previous step. Repeat this process at least 30 to 40 times.
texts.text: ¢ Bring the same pole of the magnet you started with to the same end of the sewing needle from which you began, and repeat the previous step. Repeat this process at least 30 to 40 times.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4.7(a): Making an iron needle a magnet
texts.text: Fig. 4.7(a): Making an iron needle a magnet

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Bring some iron filings or steel pins near the needle. If the pins or iron filings get attracted to the needle, then that means that the needle has become a magnet.
texts.text: ¢ Bring some iron filings or steel pins near the needle. If the pins or iron filings get attracted to the needle, then that means that the needle has become a magnet.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Pass this needle through the cork horizontally. Float the cork in a glass bow! filled with water, such that the needle always remains above the level of water as shown in Fig. 4.7b.
texts.text: @ Pass this needle through the cork horizontally. Float the cork in a glass bow! filled with water, such that the needle always remains above the level of water as shown in Fig. 4.7b.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ When the needle comes to rest, your magnetic compass is ready for use. Note the direction in which either side of the needle points.
texts.text: @ When the needle comes to rest, your magnetic compass is ready for use. Note the direction in which either side of the needle points.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4.7(b): A compass needle in a bowl of water
texts.text: Fig. 4.7(b): A compass needle in a bowl of water

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Rotate the cork gently and wait till it stops rotating. Repeat this a few more times. Do the ends of the needle always point in the same direction?
texts.text: ¢ Rotate the cork gently and wait till it stops rotating. Repeat this a few more times. Do the ends of the needle always point in the same direction?

texts.text: What happens when we bring two magnets closer to each other?

texts.orig: 4.4 Attraction and Repulsion between Magnets
texts.text: 4.4 Attraction and Repulsion between Magnets

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 4.5: Let us experiment
texts.text: Activity 4.5: Let us experiment

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Take a pair of bar magnets on which North and South poles have been marked. Mark the two bar magnets as Aand B.
texts.text: @ Take a pair of bar magnets on which North and South poles have been marked. Mark the two bar magnets as Aand B.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Place the longer side of magnet A over 5-6 round shaped pencils as shown in Fig. 4.8a.
texts.text: ¢ Place the longer side of magnet A over 5-6 round shaped pencils as shown in Fig. 4.8a.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Now bring one end of magnet B near the end of magnet A placed on the pencils. Make sure that the two magnets do not touch each other. Observe what happens.
texts.text: ¢ Now bring one end of magnet B near the end of magnet A placed on the pencils. Make sure that the two magnets do not touch each other. Observe what happens.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Next, bring the other end of magnet B near the same end of magnet A (Fig. 4.8b). Does the magnet A on the pencils begin to move? Does it always move in the direction of the approaching magnet? What do these observations suggest?
texts.text: @ Next, bring the other end of magnet B near the same end of magnet A (Fig. 4.8b). Does the magnet A on the pencils begin to move? Does it always move in the direction of the approaching magnet? What do these observations suggest?

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4.8: Interaction between two bar magnets
texts.text: Fig. 4.8: Interaction between two bar magnets

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You will see that unlike poles of two magnets, that is, the North pole of one magnet and the South pole of another magnet, attract each other. The like poles, that is, either the North poles or the South poles of both magnets, repel each other.
texts.text: You will see that unlike poles of two magnets, that is, the North pole of one magnet and the South pole of another magnet, attract each other. The like poles, that is, either the North poles or the South poles of both magnets, repel each other.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: a4 Repeat the activity by using an iron bar in place of one of the magnets. What do you observe this time? You will find that both the ends of the iron bar will be attracted by both the North and South poles ofthe magnet. From this activity, we find that a magnet can be identified by its property of repulsion.
texts.text: a4 Repeat the activity by using an iron bar in place of one of the magnets. What do you observe this time? You will find that both the ends of the iron bar will be attracted by both the North and South poles ofthe magnet. From this activity, we find that a magnet can be identified by its property of repulsion.

texts.text: Activity 4.6: Let us experiment

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: a Take a magnetic compass and a bar magnet.
texts.text: a Take a magnetic compass and a bar magnet.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: a Place the magnetic compass over a horizontal surface and wait for its needle to come to rest.
texts.text: a Place the magnetic compass over a horizontal surface and wait for its needle to come to rest.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Now slowly bring North pole of the bar magnet close to the North pole of the compass needle as shown in Fig. 4.9a. Observe the compass needle carefully. What do you observe? Does the needle deflect? If yes, in which direction?
texts.text: Now slowly bring North pole of the bar magnet close to the North pole of the compass needle as shown in Fig. 4.9a. Observe the compass needle carefully. What do you observe? Does the needle deflect? If yes, in which direction?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The compass needle is also a magnet. Will it show the same behaviour if a magnet is brought closer to it?
texts.text: The compass needle is also a magnet. Will it show the same behaviour if a magnet is brought closer to it?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Now repeat the above step with the South pole of the bar magnet. Do you observe any difference this time?
texts.text: Now repeat the above step with the South pole of the bar magnet. Do you observe any difference this time?

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4.9: A compass needle and a magnet
texts.text: Fig. 4.9: A compass needle and a magnet

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When the North pole of a magnet is brought closer to the North pole of the compass needle, it moves away as shown in Fig. 4.9a. When the South pole of the magnet is brought closer to the North pole of the compass needle, it moves closer (Fig. 4.9b).
texts.text: When the North pole of a magnet is brought closer to the North pole of the compass needle, it moves away as shown in Fig. 4.9a. When the South pole of the magnet is brought closer to the North pole of the compass needle, it moves closer (Fig. 4.9b).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Suppose we place a piece of wood between the compass needle and the magnet. Will this affect the deflection of the compass needle?
texts.text: Suppose we place a piece of wood between the compass needle and the magnet. Will this affect the deflection of the compass needle?

texts.orig: Activity 4.7: Let us investigate
texts.text: Activity 4.7: Let us investigate

texts.text: Fig. 4.10: Compass needle and a magnet with a piece of wood in between

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Repeat the first or second part of Activity 4.6.
texts.text: @ Repeat the first or second part of Activity 4.6.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Without disturbing the bar magnet and magnetic compass, place a piece of wood between them, perpendicular to the table as shown in Fig. 4.10. Observe the compass needle carefully.
texts.text: ¢ Without disturbing the bar magnet and magnetic compass, place a piece of wood between them, perpendicular to the table as shown in Fig. 4.10. Observe the compass needle carefully.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Is there any effect on the deflection of compass needle due to the piece of wood? Record your observation in Table 4.2.
texts.text: Is there any effect on the deflection of compass needle due to the piece of wood? Record your observation in Table 4.2.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Repeat the process by replacing the piece of wood with a cardboard sheet, thin plastic sheet, and a thin glass sheet.
texts.text: @ Repeat the process by replacing the piece of wood with a cardboard sheet, thin plastic sheet, and a thin glass sheet.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Table 4.2: Observing the effect of magnet through non-magnetic materials
texts.text: Table 4.2: Observing the effect of magnet through non-magnetic materials

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You would observe that there is no appreciable change in the deflection of the needle when a sheet of any of the above material is placed between the magnet and the compass needle. Therefore, we can conclude that the magnetic effect can act through non-magnetic materials.
texts.text: You would observe that there is no appreciable change in the deflection of the needle when a sheet of any of the above material is placed between the magnet and the compass needle. Therefore, we can conclude that the magnetic effect can act through non-magnetic materials.

texts.orig: A magnet has two poles—the North pole and the South pole.
texts.text: A magnet has two poles—the North pole and the South pole.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: The poles of a magnet always exist in pairs. A single North pole or a single South pole cannot exist.
texts.text: The poles of a magnet always exist in pairs. A single North pole or a single South pole cannot exist.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Magnetic materials are the materials that are attracted towards a magnet.
texts.text: Magnetic materials are the materials that are attracted towards a magnet.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Non-magnetic materials are the materials that are not attracted towards a magnet.
texts.text: Non-magnetic materials are the materials that are not attracted towards a magnet.

texts.text: The needle of a magnetic compass indicates the north-south direction.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: When two magnets are brought close to each other, like poles (North-North, South-South) repel each other while unlike poles (North-South) attract each other.
texts.text: When two magnets are brought close to each other, like poles (North-North, South-South) repel each other while unlike poles (North-South) attract each other.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: State whether the following statements are True (T) or False (F).
texts.text: State whether the following statements are True (T) or False (F).

texts.orig: (i) A magnet can be broken into pieces to obtain a single pole. [ ]

texts.orig: 5. Reshma bought three identical metal bars from the market. Out of these bars, two were magnets and one was just a piece of iron. How will she identify which two amongst the three could be magnets (without using any other material)?
texts.text: Reshma bought three identical metal bars from the market. Out of these bars, two were magnets and one was just a piece of iron. How will she identify which two amongst the three could be magnets (without using any other material)?

texts.text: You are given a magnet which does not have the poles marked. How can you find its poles with the help of another magnet which has its poles marked?

texts.label: text

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A magnetic compass is usually a small circular box with a transparent cover on it, as shown in Fig. 4.6. The magnet, in the shape of a needle, is mounted on a pin standing on the bottom of the box. This
texts.text: A magnetic compass is usually a small circular box with a transparent cover on it, as shown in Fig. 4.6. The magnet, in the shape of a needle, is mounted on a pin standing on the bottom of the box. This

texts.label: text
texts.orig: needle is balanced on the pin in sucha manner that it can move around this point easily, that is, it can rotate freely. The end of the needle which rests in the North direction is usually painted red. Below the needle, there is a dial with directions marked on it.
texts.text: needle is balanced on the pin in sucha manner that it can move around this point easily, that is, it can rotate freely. The end of the needle which rests in the North direction is usually painted red. Below the needle, there is a dial with directions marked on it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Much before the widespread use of the modern magnetic compass (Fig. 4.6), a device similar to the compass needle made by you (Fig. 4.7b) was used by Indians for navigation at sea. It consisted of a magnetised fish-shaped iron piece, kept in a vessel of oil. It was called matsya-yantra (or machchh~—=—yantra).
texts.text: Much before the widespread use of the modern magnetic compass (Fig. 4.6), a device similar to the compass needle made by you (Fig. 4.7b) was used by Indians for navigation at sea. It consisted of a magnetised fish-shaped iron piece, kept in a vessel of oil. It was called matsya-yantra (or machchh~—=—yantra).

texts.orig: Magnets can move some objects without touching them! Is that not amazing?
texts.text: Magnets can move some objects without touching them! Is that not amazing?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In some magnets, the North and South poles are marked as N and S. In some other magnets, the North pole is indicated by a white dot. Sometimes, the North pole of a magnet is painted red and South pole is painted blue.
texts.text: In some magnets, the North and South poles are marked as N and S. In some other magnets, the North pole is indicated by a white dot. Sometimes, the North pole of a magnet is painted red and South pole is painted blue.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: How to keep the magnets safe?
texts.text: How to keep the magnets safe?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Magnet says,
texts.text: Magnet says,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: "Store me properly. Keep me in pairs with unlike poles on the same side. Keep a piece of wood in between. Place two pieces of soft iron across the ends."
texts.text: "Store me properly. Keep me in pairs with unlike poles on the same side. Keep a piece of wood in between. Place two pieces of soft iron across the ends."

texts.label: text
texts.orig: "Do not heat me or drop me or hammer me.
texts.text: "Do not heat me or drop me or hammer me.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Do not keep me near mobile phones or remote controls."
texts.text: Do not keep me near mobile phones or remote controls."

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A 3 C
texts.text: A 3 C

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Magnet says "Humans have made me in different shapes and sizes as per their requirements. However, my poles always occur in pairs, no matter my shape".
texts.text: Magnet says "Humans have made me in different shapes and sizes as per their requirements. However, my poles always occur in pairs, no matter my shape".
